subject: A Review of Set Theory
question: Let A be a non-empty set and $f : A \mapsto \mathcal{P}(A)$ be any function. Let $B = \{a \in A \mid a \notin f(a)\}$. Clearly, $B \in \mathcal{P}(A)$. Show that there is no $b \in A$ such that $f(b) = B$. Hence, there is no onto map from A to $\mathcal{P}(A)$.
answer:
Suppose the contrary. That is, there is a $b \in A$ such that $f(b) = B$. Since $B \subseteq A$, either $b \in B$ or $b 
otin B$. If $b \in B$ then $b 
otin f(b)$. But $B = f(b)$ so $b \in B$ implies $b \in f(b)$, a contradiction. If $b 
otin B$ then $b \in f(b) = B$ which is again a contradiction. Hence, we conclude that there is no onto map from A to its power set.

otin f(b)$. But $B = f(b)$ so $b \in B$ implies $b \in f(b)$, a contradiction. If $b 
otin B$ then $b \in f(b) = B$ which is again a contradiction. Hence, we conclude that there is no onto map from A to its power set.
